journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society

title-group.article-title: The Association of Long COVID‐19 Symptoms, Physical Function, and Activities of Daily Living Among Older Women

aff.named-content: Department of Internal Medicine, College of Medicine
School of Public Health and Health Professions
Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science and Division of Geriatrics, Gerontology, and Palliative Care, Department of Medicine

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Journal of the American Geriatrics Society published by Wiley Periodicals LLC on behalf of The American Geriatrics Society.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

related-article.article-title: Post‐Discharge COVID‐19 Symptoms Predict 1‐Year Functional Decline, Falls, and Emergency Department Visits: A Cohort Study
related-article.source: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The impact of COVID‐19 on physical function (PF) outcomes among older adults remains unclear. We examined the long‐term association between COVID, PF, and Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) among women from the Women's Health Initiative (WHI).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Participants from the WHI who completed the COVID‐19 survey (2021–2022) and annual survey (2022) were included. Self‐reported data on COVID‐19 testing and symptoms (2021–2022) were used. PF score and ADLs were evaluated pre‐ and post‐COVID‐19 survey by the 36‐Item Short Form Survey PF subscale, the Lawton Instrumental Activities of Daily Living, and the Katz Index of Independence in ADL. Multivariable linear regression and logistic regression were used and adjusted for pre‐COVID functioning to examine the association between COVID status, PF, and ADLs. The interaction between pre‐COVID functioning and COVID status was tested.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Among the 13,933 WHI participants, 71.4% were aged ≥ 80 years, and 88.6% were Non‐Hispanic White. Only 8.7% tested positive for COVID‐19 (n = 1210), with 35.1% having long COVID (n = 425). The most common long COVID symptoms were fatigue (18.2%), malaise (12.2%), memory problems (12.1%), and brain fog (11.2%). Women who tested COVID+ had lower PF scores (60 vs. 65, p = 0.045) and were less likely to be able to do all ADLs without help (74% vs. 79.2%, p = 0.015) compared to those who never tested COVID+. After controlling for covariates, post‐COVID PF scores did not differ by COVID status (p = 0.30), although pre‐COVID PF scores were significantly linked to post‐COVID scores (p < 0.001). Similarly, the odds of being able to do all ADLs without any help did not differ by COVID status (p = 0.31), with pre‐COVID ADLs significantly associated with post‐COVID ADLs (p < 0.001).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In older women, after accounting for pre‐COVID functional status, the association between long COVID and lower functioning became nonsignificant. Our findings highlight the importance of preserving physical functioning among older women.

institution-wrap.institution: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

fn.p: Funding: This work was supported by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute, National Institutes of Health, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services through contracts (HHSN268201600018C, HHSN268201600001C, HHSN268201600002C, HSN268201600003C, and HHSN268201600004C). This work was supported by National Cancer Institute (K00CA253745 to X.Z.).

list-item.p:
Key points
Among older women who tested positive for COVID‐19, more than one‐third experienced long COVID.
These older women reported significantly lower physical function scores compared to those who never tested positive for COVID‐19. However, no significant association was found between COVID‐19 and Activities of Daily Living (ADL).
After adjusting for pre‐COVID functional status, the link between long COVID and lower functioning became non‐significant, emphasizing the importance of preserving physical function in older women.

Why does this paper matter?
Pre‐COVID functional status is associated with physical function and ADL, regardless of long COVID status, after COVID among older women.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The World Health Organization defines long‐term symptoms from COVID‐19 as long COVID if the symptoms and effects last for at least 2 months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis [1, 2]. Using a clinically informed approach, some have suggested clustering the COVID symptoms into various groups to better describe the multifactorial nature of the condition [3, 4]. Long COVID can manifest differently for individuals, with impacts on multiple organ systems, including the heart, lungs, immune system, pancreas, gastrointestinal tract, neurological system, kidneys, blood vessels, and reproductive system [5, 6, 7]. Older age, female sex, and the presence of underlying chronic diseases (e.g., type 2 diabetes, autoimmune diseases, connective tissue disorders) are risk factors for long COVID [8, 9]. However, the impact of long COVID in older populations is unknown.

After a COVID‐19 diagnosis, the most common functional consequences include respiratory dysfunction and motor impairment, both of which are critical to functional status for older adults [10, 11]. Mobility is an essential component of well‐being and an important determinant of healthy aging [12]. The impact of COVID‐19 on mobility and functioning outcomes, however, is not clear, especially among nonhospitalized community‐dwelling older adults [12]. Among adults with COVID‐19 infections who had a clinic visit, those who had long COVID experienced a negative impact on physical function and daily activities [13, 14]. Recent research also highlighted the importance of being able to complete ADLs as a normal part of resuming life after long COVID [15]. Individuals with severe COVID‐19 reported ongoing symptoms and physical limitations after hospitalization that lasted up to several months after discharge [16, 17]. Even mild to moderate COVID‐19 can have negative ongoing functional associations for nonhospitalized individuals [12]. A recent review summarized the current management of long COVID among older people as geriatric rehabilitation [18]. However, no emphasis on the functional status or performance of ADLs was included. Therefore, the objective of this study was to examine the association between long COVID, physical function (PF), and activities of daily living (ADLs) among older women using the Women's Health Initiative (WHI) Cohort.

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: The WHI is a longitudinal study of 161,808 postmenopausal women recruited from 40 clinical centers across the US. Study details have been described previously [19, 20]. Briefly, postmenopausal women (aged 50–79 years) were enrolled between 1993 and 1998 at 40 clinical centers in one or more randomized clinical trials (WHI‐CT, n = 68,132) or an observational study (WHI‐OS, n = 93,676). The WHI collected extensive self‐reported data on demographics, medical history, diet, medication, and dietary supplement use, psychosocial and behavioral measures, and selected environmental factors. All participants provided written informed consent initially and for the extension periods, and Institutional Review Boards of the participating WHI clinical centers and the clinical coordinating center approved the study protocols.

In response to the COVID‐19 pandemic, the WHI investigators collected a series of COVID‐19 surveys (COVID‐19 Survey 1, June–December 2020; COVID‐19 Survey 2, August 2021–February 2022), queried active WHI participants on various experiences relating to the COVID‐19 pandemic (https://www.whi.org/doc/WHI‐COVID‐19‐Survey.pdf; https://www.whi.org/doc/F191‐v1.pdf). The current study included women who completed the COVID‐19 Survey 2 and reported that they were tested for COVID‐19 (n = 13,933) and knew whether they were COVID‐negative (n = 12,723) or COVID‐positive and had COVID symptom data (n = 1210).

sec.title: Measures
sec.p: Testing for COVID‐19 was self‐reported in the COVID‐19 Survey 2 (2021–2022). Women were asked whether they had taken a COVID‐19 test, the test result, the presence of any COVID‐19 symptoms, and the duration of each symptom (SI Table S1). Long COVID was defined as any COVID‐19 symptom lasting ≥ 8 weeks according to the World Health Organization's definition [1, 2]. Participants who reported any COVID‐19 symptoms that last at least 8 weeks were classified as “had long COVID”. Participants with missing data on presence and duration of COVID symptoms were excluded. Long COVID‐19 symptoms were classified into five clusters (SI Table S1): core symptoms, core symptoms + loss of smell/taste symptoms, nonspecific viral symptoms, nonspecific viral symptoms + sleep disturbance, and cognitive symptoms [3, 4].

Data on age at COVID‐19 Survey 2, race, ethnicity, education, household income, U.S. region resided in at the time of COVID‐19 survey, marital status, insurance type, rural/urban residence, and the number of comorbidities (including hypertension, heart disease, stroke, hyperlipidemia, cancer, depression, osteoporosis, asthma, diabetes, arthritis) were self‐reported [21].

A 10‐item physical functioning subscale of the RAND 36‐Item Health Survey was obtained from the 2022 WHI annual follow‐up questionnaire, which was collected after the COVID‐19 Survey 2 [22]. Participants were asked, “Does your health now limit you in these activities, and if so, how much?” Activities included vigorous activities, moderate activities, carrying groceries, climbing one/several flight(s) of stairs, bending/kneeling/stooping, walking more than a mile, walking one/several block(s), and bathing/dressing yourself. Response options were “not limited at all” = 3, “limited a little” = 2, or “limited a lot” = 1. Physical functioning (PF) scores were scaled to a range from 0 (low) to 100 (high) functioning [23]. It is a norm‐based scoring with mean score of 50 and a standard deviation of 10. The minimal clinical important difference (MCID) for the PF range from 2 to 5 points depending on the study populations [24, 25].

A six‐item activities of daily living scale (ADLs) was also assessed on the 2022 WHI annual follow‐up questionnaire, derived from the Lawton Instrumental ADL and the Katz Index of Independence in ADL [26, 27]. Participants were asked how much help (if any) they needed to do routine activities, including feeding themselves, dressing, getting in and out of bed, taking a bath or shower, doing their own grocery shopping, and keeping track of and taking medicines. Participants could choose from “by myself without help”, “with some help”, and “completely unable to do by myself”. The composite ADL score ranges from 0 to 6. A score of 6 indicates that the participant was able to do all activities alone with no help, whereas scores of 5 or less indicate needing some help or being unable to perform at least one ADL.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Descriptive statistics were reported overall, as well as stratified by women who had never tested positive for COVID, who tested positive for COVID without long COVID, and who tested positive for COVID with long COVID. Frequencies and Chi‐Square tests were used to compare the distribution of levels of covariates between groups. We summarized the numbers and proportions of women who were tested for COVID‐19, tested positive for COVID‐19, and had long COVID, as well as long COVID symptoms and symptom clusters.

For post‐COVID PF and ADLs, we linked the functional data from the 2022 WHI annual follow‐up questionnaire among women who had COVID‐19 testing data. Pre‐COVID PF and ADLs data were approximated by using the individual's latest measures that were at least 2 years prior to the COVID‐19 Survey 2. The median interval between pre‐COVID data collection and the COVID‐19 Survey 2 functional assessment was 2.7 years (range: 2.0–8.6). We described the median, range of lower to upper quartile for PF scores, and numbers and proportions for women who were able to do all ADLs without any help. Univariate comparisons were made using Wilcoxon rank‐sum tests for PF scores and chi‐square tests for ADLs. To examine the difference in PF scores between women who had never tested positive for COVID, COVID positive with and without long COVID, we used linear regression models and reported least square means and 95% confidence intervals (95% CI). To examine the difference in ADLs between women who had never tested positive for COVID, COVID positive with and without long COVID, we used logistic regression models to calculate odds ratios (OR) and 95% CI for being able to do all ADLs without any help (ADL score = 6) among the three groups. Multivariable models were adjusted for age at COVID survey 2, race/ethnicity, education, income, U.S. region, marital status, insurance type, rural/metro residence, and number of comorbidities, and the pre‐COVID PF or ADLs score. Interaction analyses of COVID status X pre‐COVID PF score (or ADLs) and COVID status X age were conducted to examine whether the association between COVID, PF, and ADLs differed by pre‐COVID functioning and age.

Similar analyses were conducted to examine whether a long COVID symptom cluster was associated with PF and ADLs. For each long COVID symptom cluster, we compared women with the long COVID symptom cluster, women who had long COVID without the symptom cluster, and women who had no long COVID. All statistical tests were two‐sided, and alpha was set at p < 0.05. The statistical analyses were performed from January to May 2023 using SAS version 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc. Cary, NC).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: There were 13,933 WHI participants who reported that they were tested for COVID‐19 (Table 1). Most women were 80–90 years old (57.5%), White (88.7%), not Hispanic or Latino (96.7%), had some college or an associate degree (34.3%), married or living as married (71.4%), had private insurance (79.0%), lived in urban areas (92.3%), and had two or more comorbidities (71.2%). A total of 12,723 women (91.3%) had never tested positive for COVID, while 1237 (8.7%) tested positive for COVID. Among those who had COVID and reported COVID symptoms (n = 1210), 785 (64.9%) did not have long COVID, and 425 (35.1%) reported long COVID symptoms.

caption.p: Characteristics of Participants by COVID Status in the Women's Health Initiative.

fn.p: p‐Value was from the comparison among women who had never tested positive, tested COVID positive with and without long COVID.

sec.title: Long COVID Symptoms
sec.p: The most common long COVID symptom was fatigue (18.2%), followed by malaise (12.2%), memory problems (12.1%), brain fog (11.2%), confusion or difficulty thinking or concentrating (10.4%), loss of smell (9.9%), shortness of breath (9%), and loss of taste (8.3%). Fast‐beating heart (2.7%), chest pain/tightness (1.7%), and fever (0.7%) were the least common long COVID symptoms (SI Table S2). For long COVID symptom clusters, 69.4% of women who had long COVID experienced non‐specific viral symptoms (any headache, muscle pain, joint pain, fatigue, shortness of breath, malaise), 47.8% experienced cognitive symptoms (any memory problems, confusion or difficulty thinking or concentrating, brain fog), 19.5% had core symptoms (any cough, fever, chest pain), 18.8% experienced non‐specific viral symptoms with sleep disturbance, and 5.7% experienced core symptoms with loss of taste or smell.

sec.title: Associations Between COVID, Long COVID, and Physical Function
sec.p: Women who tested COVID‐positive had lower PF scores compared to women who had never tested positive (Figure 1, Table 2. Median: 60 vs. 65, p = 0.04). After further classification, women who tested COVID positive with long COVID compared to women who had never tested positive had lower PF scores (median: 55 vs. 65), whereas women who tested COVID positive without long COVID had the same median PF scores as those who had never tested positive (median: 65). After adjusting for other covariates, compared to women who had never tested positive, women with long COVID had 1.18 points lower PF score (Figure 2, SI Table S3. LS Mean = −1.18, 95% CI: −4.24, 1.89), but this was not significantly different, neither were PF scores among women who tested COVID positive without long COVID (LS Mean = −1.62, 95% CI: −3.91, 0.67). The pre‐COVID PF scores, however, were significantly associated with PF score (LS Mean = 0.81, 95% CI: 0.79, 0.83). When further exploring whether pre‐COVID PF score modified the association between COVID status and PF score at COVID‐19 survey 2, a significant interaction between pre‐COVID PF score (> 80) and COVID status was found (p < 0.001). Specifically, among women who had a higher pre‐COVID PF score, the differences in PF score at COVID‐19 survey 2 between those who tested COVID positive with long COVID vs. those who had never tested positive for COVID were greater than the differences between women who tested positive for COVID without long COVID (SI Table S4, Figure 3). Whereas, for women who had a lower pre‐COVID PF score (≤ 80), the differences in PF score at COVID‐19 survey 2 between those who tested COVID positive with and without long COVID compared to those who had never tested positive for COVID were smaller and not significant. The association between COVID status and physical functioning did not differ by age group (p = 0.13, data not shown).

caption.p: Physical function and activities of daily living (ADLs) among women who never tested COVID positive, who tested COVID+ without long COVID, and who tested COVID+ with long COVID.
Physical function score and activities of daily living among women's health initiative participants.

tr.th: Able to perform all activities without any help (n, %)

caption.p: Forest plot: multivariable model of examining the associations of long COVID with physical function (PF).
Predicted physical function score at COVID‐19 survey 2 by COVID status from a multivariable model with the interaction between COVID status and pre‐COVID physical function score.

sec.title: Associations Between COVID, Long COVID, and Activities of Daily Living
sec.p: Women who tested positive for COVID were less likely to be able to do all ADLs without any help compared to women who had never tested positive for COVID (Figure 1, Table 2. 74.0% vs. 79.2%, p = 0.02). After further analysis, the percentage of those able to do all ADLs without any help was lowest among women who tested COVID positive with long COVID, followed by women who tested COVID positive without long COVID, and highest among women who had never tested positive for COVID (72.5% vs. 74.8% vs. 79.2%, p = 0.045). After adjusting for other covariates, however, the odds of being able to perform all ADLs without any help did not differ among women who had never tested positive for COVID and women who tested COVID positive with and without long COVID (SI Table S3; OR with long COVID = 0.69, 95% CI: 0.42, 1.12; OR without long COVID = 0.95, 95% CI: 0.65, 1.38). Pre‐COVID ADL score was associated with post‐COVID ability to do all ADLs without help (OR = 28.47, 95% CI: 21.90, 37.02). The association between COVID status and ADLs did not differ by pre‐COVID PF score (p = 0.54, data not shown) and age group (p = 0.88, data not shown). No association between long COVID symptom clusters and PF or ADLs was observed (SI Table S5).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study demonstrated that among older women who tested positive for COVID‐19, more than one‐third experienced long COVID. Women who experienced long COVID reported significantly lower physical function scores compared to those who never tested positive for COVID‐19. However, no significant association was found between COVID‐19 and ADL. After adjusting for pre‐COVID functional status, the link between long COVID and lower functioning became non‐significant, emphasizing the importance of preserving physical function in older women. Specifically, we found that the difference in PF score between women who tested COVID positive with long COVID and who had never tested COVID positive was greater among women who had higher pre‐COVID PF scores, as opposed to women who had lower pre‐COVID PF scores. This indicated that women who had higher PF scores pre‐COVID may experience a greater impact from long COVID on physical functioning. On the other hand, although the percentage of women who were able to perform all ADLs without help was smaller among those who had long COVID symptoms, there was no significant association between COVID‐19 and ADLs ability. Long COVID is a multisystemic illness that may have long‐term sequelae. Despite therapeutic clinical trials and other research to develop effective treatments for long COVID, the current treatment options for long COVID are only based on relieving shared symptoms from other diseases, and no effective treatment has been identified to combat long COVID [9]. Therefore, conducting regular screenings for physical function and ADLs among older adults might provide opportunities to intervene through physical therapy, strength training, and dietary modification to preserve functional status for this population. The emphasis on addressing functional status among older adults could also be achieved through research that builds upon current knowledge from patient experience, as well as offers training and education to healthcare providers and research professionals, with the goal of improving both care and the quality of life for those affected by long COVID.

Long COVID occurs across all age groups and symptom severity, with prevalence ranging from 10% to 71% [28, 29]. Moreover, compared to men, women are more likely to experience long COVID [30]. Our study found that 35% of women who tested COVID+ experienced long COVID symptoms, which is slightly higher than the reported 30.2% of individuals from a population‐representative survey in the U.S. [31]. This may be due to the older age and greater number of comorbidities of the WHI participants (more than 70% had at least two comorbidities), both of which are the aforementioned risk factors for long COVID.

In WHI, the most common long COVID symptoms were fatigue, malaise, memory problems, brain fog, and confusion or difficulty thinking or concentrating. This is in line with previous studies that show long COVID increases the risk of chronic fatigue and affects cognitive function [5, 6, 32]. Furthermore, long COVID symptoms, such as psychotic disorders and cognitive impairments, can persist for 2 years or longer after COVID infection, leading to a poor quality of life [28]. A study showed that the ability to perform daily activities and COVID‐related pain/discomfort improved over time, which was consistent with our findings that long COVID was not associated with needing help with ADLs [28]. The same study pointed out that concentration difficulties and amnesia continued to be substantial following the infection [28]. Given that a significant proportion of individuals experienced long COVID, with its associated symptoms persisting for an extended time, it is crucial to consider long COVID as a long‐term health burden, even among those who had mild infections. However, most of the studies on long COVID and morbidity did not account for pre‐COVID morbidity and functional status. Our study was able to maximize pre‐COVID functioning data to understand whether and how pre‐COVID functional status modified the association between COVID status and functioning post COVID. Our findings highlight the importance of using pre‐COVID data and the urgent needs of regular functional assessments for older women, even among those who had higher scores pre‐COVID, to address the substantial functioning decline after long COVID. Future studies with multiple repeated measures for pre‐ and post‐long COVID are needed to understand the physiological mechanisms of long COVID symptoms and symptom clusters in terms of long‐term functional outcomes.

COVID‐19 can affect skeletal muscles, and individuals infected with COVID‐19 may experience incomplete recovery of physical function and long‐term impairments 1–2 years post‐infection [33]. One study assessed post‐COVID sarcopenia among older adults (≥ 65 years). Measured by dynamometry, 85% of women had possible sarcopenia 3 months post‐COVID infection. Although the number dropped, 67% of women still had possible sarcopenia 12 months post‐COVID infection [34]. Muscle strength has been shown to improve over time after COVID infection. However, the impact on functioning has been shown to remain substantial [33]. In our study, compared to women who had never tested COVID positive, PF score among women who tested COVID positive with long COVID was significantly lower, whereas PF score among women who tested COVID positive without long COVID showed no difference. Moreover, the differences in how COVID status impacts physical functioning that we observed after accounting for pre‐COVID functioning status were fascinating. The underlying physiological mechanisms that could explain these differences are unclear; however, pre‐COVID functional status may lead to variations in declines of muscle strength. Physical function can predict the risk of falls and mortality in older individuals, suggesting a critical need to address physical functional declines among older populations [35, 36]. Even for those who had higher physical functioning pre‐COVID, regular clinical assessments of physical function are needed to monitor substantial changes in functional status. By referring individuals to physical therapy and encouraging practical exercise routines among this older population, it is possible that some individuals may preserve their physical functioning and improve overall mobility [37].

Our study has several strengths. We considered longitudinal measures of health in a uniquely older population of U.S. women. Our analysis was able to capture health conditions prior to COVID‐19 and utilize measurements of PF and ADLs prior to and after COVID‐19 infection, which allowed us to explore the interaction between pre‐COVID functioning and COVID status on PF and ADLs. The WHI is a geographically diverse population with rich data on health, lifestyle, and behaviors. Additionally, most studies focused on long COVID collected data from patients who sought medical care, treatment, or were hospitalized for COVID. Our study adds to the literature with an older population that was not specifically hospitalized or medically treated for COVID [38, 39, 40, 41]. This study also has the additional strength of repeated measures across the same participants.

Our study has some limitations. All COVID‐19 outcomes were self‐reported, and medical records were not reviewed for confirmation. Additionally, the timing of COVID positive cases cannot be determined, which may need to be considered based on the multiple COVID‐19 waves of infection and symptomology changes with variants of the original COVID‐19 strain. It is possible that women who were infected more than once may have different outcomes, especially among the older population. However, we were unable to determine if our study population had multiple COVID‐19 infections. It is also likely that long COVID was under‐reported. Most patients with COVID‐19 from the first waves did not have laboratory‐confirmed infections, with PCR tests being difficult to access unless individuals were hospitalized. Only 1%–3% of cases prior to March 2020 were likely detected [42], and only 25% of cases in the USA were reported from February 2020 to September 2021 [43]; that percentage has likely decreased with the rise in the use of at‐home rapid tests. The WHI collected COVID survey 2 between 2021 and 2022. Our findings on the prevalence of long COVID only applied to individuals who had COVID during that time period.

Furthermore, this study focused on the cross‐sectional relationship between long COVID and functioning. Our findings demonstrated that long COVID was associated with lower PF and ADLs score, however, after accounting the pre‐COVID PF, the associations of long COVID attenuated. Due to the study design and lack of data on the timing of COVID infections, we are not able to determine the causality between pre‐COVID functional status and long COVID. It is possible that women who had lower functional status pre‐COVID were more likely to report long COVID symptoms. Future studies with detailed information on COVID infections should examine the longitudinal changes of functioning before and after long COVID. The study population also needs to be considered. We had a sample of older (71% ≥ 80 years old), mostly Non‐Hispanic White (88.7%) women, suggesting that our results may not be generalizable to other populations. Also, our population had a high percentage of comorbidities, with 71.2% having two or more common conditions. Survival bias also must be taken into consideration, as these participants had to be healthy enough to complete the survey. We acknowledge that the definition of long COVID has evolved since 2021, including the definition from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine: “long COVID is an infection‐asscoiated chronic condition that occurs after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and is present for at least 3 months as a continuous, relapsing and remitting, or progressive disease state that affects one or more organ systems” [44]. This study opted to use the 2021 WHO definition since that was closer to the timing of the WHI COVID Survey 2. Finally, we did not have information on receipt of the COVID vaccine in relation to the timing of COVID testing. Previous studies reported findings on COVID vaccination and risk of long COVID have not been consistent; some studies showed that vaccination before or after COVID‐19 reduces the risk of developing long COVID [31, 45], while others found no association between COVID vaccination, the number of vaccinations received, and the risk of long COVID [38, 39].

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: We observed a marked differences in physical functioning among older women who tested positive for COVID‐19 with long COVID. The differences in physical functioning between women who tested positive for COVID with long COVID and those who had never tested positive for COVID were greater among women who had higher pre‐COVID physical functioning scores compared to those who had lower pre‐COVID physical functioning scores.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Xiaochen Zhang: conceptualization, formal analysis, methodology, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing. Chloe Hery: conceptualization, formal analysis, methodology, writing – review and editing. Eric M. McLaughlin: conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis, methodology, writing – review and editing. Nancy F. Woods, Marian L. Neuhouser, Holly Harris, Emily W. Gower, Jean Wactawski‐Wende, Aladdin H. Shadyab, Robert B. Wallace: methodology, writing – review and editing. Electra D. Paskett: conceptualization, funding acquisition, supervision, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: Electra Paskett would like to disclose that she has grant funding from the Merck Foundation, Genentech, Guardant Health, AstraZeneca, and Pfizer. All other authors report that there are no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Linked Article
sec.p: This publication is linked to a related article by Dias et al. To view this article, visit https://doi.org/10.1111/jgs.19490.

Supplemental Table 3. Multivariable models examining associations between Long COVID or various symptom clusters with Physical Functioning and Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) in women who tested positive for COVID in the Women's Health Initiative.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors have nothing to report.
